sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Vascular dementia is the second most common dementia; it is characterized by STEPWISE decline (discrete steps corresponding to vascular events) rather than the gradual decline of AD.
VaD disproportionately impairs EXECUTIVE FUNCTION (planning, organizing, decision-making) rather than episodic memory (which fails first in AD).
Gait disturbance, urge incontinence, and pseudobulbar affect appear EARLY in VaD — unlike AD where they occur in late stages.
Hypertension is the single strongest modifiable risk factor — aggressive BP management (target < 130/80) is the most important intervention for both prevention and slowing progression.

Atrial fibrillation: anticoagulation with DOACs reduces cardioembolic stroke risk by 60–70% — withholding anticoagulation requires formal risk-benefit assessment, not blanket avoidance due to fall risk.
Post-stroke depression affects 30–50% of VaD patients and is due to structural brain injury, not simply psychological reaction — requires active SSRI treatment.
Unlike Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia progression can be significantly modified by aggressive vascular risk factor management — this is the most clinically important distinction.
FAST stroke recognition education is mandatory for every VaD patient and family: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech problems, Time to call 911.

pretest.stem: A 74-year-old man has a history of three documented TIAs over 5 years. His family reports that after each TIA, 'he was never quite the same as before.' His MoCA shows difficulty with clock drawing, Trails B, and digit span, but his delayed recall is near-normal. He has a history of hypertension (currently uncontrolled) and type 2 diabetes. Which type of dementia does this presentation MOST suggest?

options.A: Alzheimer's disease
options.B: Lewy body dementia
options.C: Frontotemporal dementia
options.D: Vascular dementia

pretest.rationale: Classic vascular dementia profile: (1) stepwise deterioration following each TIA ('never quite the same after each event'); (2) cognitive pattern showing EXECUTIVE FUNCTION impairment (Trails B, clock drawing, digit span) with RELATIVELY PRESERVED RECALL — the opposite of AD; (3) vascular risk factor burden (uncontrolled hypertension, diabetes); (4) three documented TIA events. This presentation is pathognomonic for vascular dementia.

distractorRationales.A: AD typically has insidious gradual onset, episodic memory failing first (recall impaired), no stepwise pattern, and normal neurological exam.
distractorRationales.B: LBD would present with fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and RBD — none of these are described.
distractorRationales.C: Frontotemporal dementia would show behavioral/personality changes or language impairment before executive dysfunction becomes the sole cognitive deficit; no TIA history.

pretest.hint: Stepwise + post-TIA + executive dysfunction + vascular risk factors = ?
pretest.clinicalPearl: The MoCA executive function items (Trails B, clock drawing, letter fluency) are disproportionately impaired in VaD while delayed recall is relatively spared — the opposite pattern from AD.
pretest.examStrategy: VaD pattern: stepwise decline + executive dysfunction + vascular risk factors. AD pattern: insidious + memory first. Know both — they are the highest-yield differentiating features.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Stepwise cognitive decline following vascular events + executive dysfunction + vascular risk factors = vascular dementia.

pretest.stem: A 78-year-old woman with recently diagnosed vascular dementia has atrial fibrillation. Her daughter asks the nurse, 'Should my mother be on a blood thinner if she has dementia and falls sometimes?' What is the MOST evidence-based response?

options.A: 'Patients with dementia should never be anticoagulated — the bleeding risk is too high'
options.B: 'Anticoagulation is important to prevent strokes in AF; a structured risk-benefit assessment weighing stroke risk (CHADS2-VASc) against bleeding risk is needed — dementia alone is not a reason to withhold it'
options.C: 'Your mother's dementia is already caused by AF, so anticoagulation won't help now'
options.D: 'Aspirin is sufficient blood thinning for AF in older adults with dementia'

pretest.rationale: Evidence-based anticoagulation decision-making in AF: (1) AF increases ischemic stroke risk 5× and contributes to further dementia progression; (2) anticoagulation (DOACs) reduces this risk by 60–70%; (3) dementia itself does not increase hemorrhagic stroke or major bleeding risk; (4) a history of some falls does not automatically outweigh stroke prevention benefit — the annualized stroke risk from AF (often 5–10% per year) must be compared to hemorrhagic risk; (5) formal tools (CHADS2-VASc for stroke risk, HAS-BLED for bleeding risk) guide individualized decision. Blanket withholding of anticoagulation based on dementia diagnosis is not evidence-based.

distractorRationales.A: Dementia is not a contraindication to anticoagulation. This is a common but evidence-deficient clinical practice.
distractorRationales.C: While past strokes may have contributed to VaD, AF continues to carry ongoing stroke risk — prevention of further events is still clinically important.
distractorRationales.D: Aspirin is NOT an adequate substitute for anticoagulation in AF — clinical trials (BAFTA, AVERROES) show anticoagulants are significantly superior and aspirin provides minimal protection against cardioembolic stroke.

pretest.hint: Anticoagulation decision: weigh stroke risk against bleeding risk using validated tools — dementia alone is not a veto.
pretest.clinicalPearl: The most common clinical error in AF management in dementia: withholding anticoagulation because 'they fall' without formal risk-benefit quantification. One fall per year carries lower injury probability than 5–10% annual stroke risk in high CHADS2-VASc score patients.
pretest.examStrategy: Anticoagulation in AF with dementia questions: the answer supports evidence-based risk-benefit assessment, not blanket withholding. DOAC preferred over warfarin.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Dementia alone is not a contraindication to anticoagulation in AF — structured risk-benefit assessment using validated tools guides the individualized decision.

pretest.stem: A nurse is assessing a 71-year-old man with moderate vascular dementia who seems depressed. He scores 9/15 on the GDS-15, is not eating, stays in his room, and says 'What's the point of anything anymore?' His wife says 'He cried for an hour yesterday when we watched a sad commercial on TV.' Which of the following statements BEST explains his presentation?

options.A: Depression and pseudobulbar affect are independent phenomena that can both co-occur in vascular dementia, and both require assessment and management
options.B: Crying at a commercial confirms clinical depression and antidepressant therapy should be started immediately
options.C: The crying at a commercial indicates he has no real depression — it is only pseudobulbar affect
options.D: GDS-15 is not valid in patients with vascular dementia and should not be used

pretest.rationale: This patient shows BOTH: (1) Clinical depression — GDS-15 of 9/15 (positive screen ≥5), anorexia, social withdrawal, hopelessness; and (2) Pseudobulbar affect (PBA) — excessive, disproportionate emotional responses (crying for an hour over a TV commercial) caused by structural disruption of corticobulbar tracts controlling emotional expression. These are distinct phenomena with different treatments: depression responds to SSRIs/psychotherapy; PBA responds to dextromethorphan/quinidine (Nuedexta) or also SSRIs at lower doses. Both can co-occur and both require treatment.

distractorRationales.B: Crying at a commercial is PBA, not direct evidence of depression — though both are present. Isolating one sign is insufficient clinical reasoning.
distractorRationales.C: PBA and depression can and frequently do co-exist in VaD. The GDS-15 score of 9 and the anorexia and withdrawal are independent evidence of depression.
distractorRationales.D: GDS-15 is validated for use in mild-moderate dementia including vascular dementia.

pretest.hint: Depression and pseudobulbar affect are different conditions that can coexist in VaD — distinguish and treat both.
pretest.clinicalPearl: Pseudobulbar affect (PBA): the key feature is disproportionality — the emotional response is far out of proportion to the trigger. Crying for an hour at a commercial is PBA; crying when discussing a loss is grief.
pretest.examStrategy: VaD emotional symptoms: learn to distinguish depression (consistent low mood, anhedonia, GDS score) from pseudobulbar affect (sudden, disproportionate, uncontrollable emotional outbursts). Both can be present simultaneously.
pretest.keyTakeaway: VaD: depression (SSRIs) and pseudobulbar affect (Nuedexta or SSRIs) are distinct conditions that co-occur in 30–50% of patients — screen and treat both.

pretest.stem: A nurse is developing a secondary prevention care plan for a 68-year-old woman with mild vascular cognitive impairment following a left lacunar infarct. Her vascular risk factors include hypertension (BP 158/96 today), hyperlipidemia, and she smokes 1 pack/day. She is currently not anticoagulated. ECG shows normal sinus rhythm. Which of the following interventions are evidence-based priorities? Select all that apply.

options.A: Initiate or optimize antihypertensive therapy to target BP < 130/80
options.B: Prescribe anticoagulation therapy (warfarin or DOAC) for non-cardioembolic stroke prevention
options.C: Counsel on smoking cessation; offer NRT, behavioral support, and pharmacotherapy
options.D: Optimize statin therapy to target LDL-C < 1.8 mmol/L (< 70 mg/dL)
options.E: Initiate antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81 mg or clopidogrel 75 mg daily)
options.F: Recommend aerobic exercise 150 minutes/week as both vascular risk reduction and potential cognitive benefit

pretest.rationale: (A) BP control to < 130/80 is the highest-priority secondary prevention intervention. (C) Smoking cessation is immediately protective against further vascular events. (D) Statin therapy reduces recurrent stroke risk and addresses hyperlipidemia. (E) Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel) is standard for non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke/TIA secondary prevention. (F) Regular aerobic exercise reduces vascular risk and has evidence for cognitive benefit. Anticoagulation (B) is indicated for CARDIOEMBOLIC stroke prevention (i.e., atrial fibrillation) — NOT for small vessel lacunar infarction. Anticoagulation for lacunar stroke has no benefit and increases hemorrhagic risk.

distractorRationales.B: Anticoagulation is NOT indicated for small vessel lacunar infarction in normal sinus rhythm. It is reserved for cardioembolic sources (AF, mechanical heart valves, etc.). Using anticoagulation here increases bleeding risk without benefit.

pretest.hint: Lacunar stroke + normal sinus rhythm = antiplatelet, not anticoagulation. Anticoagulate only for cardioembolic sources.
pretest.clinicalPearl: Antiplatelet vs anticoagulation decision: always determine the stroke mechanism first. Lacunar (small vessel) = antiplatelet. Cardioembolic (AF) = anticoagulate.
pretest.examStrategy: Stroke prevention SATA: antiplatelet for non-cardioembolic; anticoagulation for AF. Anticoagulation for lacunar stroke = incorrect. BP + statin + exercise + smoking cessation = always correct.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Lacunar stroke secondary prevention: antiplatelet (not anticoagulant) + BP control + statin + smoking cessation + exercise.

pretest.stem: A 79-year-old man with moderate vascular dementia lives at home with his son. His son reports: 'Dad's gait has gotten much worse over the past few months — he shuffles, barely lifts his feet, and we have had three near-falls.' His MoCA is 19/30. His walking pattern is observed to be slow, narrow-based, with very small shuffling steps. What is the MOST appropriate nursing action?

options.A: Attribute the gait changes to normal aging and continue monitoring
options.B: Refer to physiotherapy for formal gait and balance assessment, assistive device fitting, and home safety evaluation
options.C: Initiate a fall protocol with bilateral wrist restraints when the patient is standing
options.D: Prescribe levodopa for presumed Parkinson's disease contributing to the gait changes

pretest.rationale: Gait disturbance is a characteristic EARLY feature of vascular dementia (subcortical white matter disease disrupts frontal motor programming — producing 'marche à petit pas': small steps, shuffling, feet barely lifting — also called magnetic gait or frontal gait disorder). This gait pattern is NOT normal aging and is NOT benign — it is a major fall risk. Physiotherapy referral for formal assessment and appropriate assistive device fitting (typically a rollator walker for this pattern) is the evidence-based intervention. Gait training reduces fall risk. Restraints are contraindicated. Prescribing levodopa requires physician diagnosis and prescription — nurses do not prescribe.

distractorRationales.A: Magnetic gait with near-falls is NOT normal aging — it is a VaD symptom requiring intervention.
distractorRationales.C: Physical restraints are contraindicated for gait instability — they cause injury, restrict rehabilitation, and do not prevent falls.
distractorRationales.D: Prescribing medication is outside nursing scope of practice. Additionally, magnetic gait from VaD does not typically respond to levodopa (unlike PD parkinsonism).

pretest.hint: Magnetic gait in VaD = early, prominent, major fall risk. Physiotherapy is the first intervention.
pretest.clinicalPearl: The frontal gait disorder of VaD ('magnetic gait' or 'marche à petit pas') is pathologically different from Parkinsonian gait — it responds to gait training and assistive devices but NOT to levodopa.
pretest.examStrategy: Gait disturbance questions in VaD: the nursing action is physiotherapy referral + fall prevention. Never restraints. Medication prescription is never a nursing action.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Magnetic gait in VaD is an early, major fall risk feature — physiotherapy referral for gait training and assistive device is the evidence-based nursing action.

pretest.stem: Which of the following best describes the typical course of vascular dementia?

options.A: Gradual, insidious progressive decline over many years
options.B: Fluctuating cognition with dramatic variation from hour to hour
options.C: Stepwise deterioration with discrete episodes of decline followed by periods of relative stability
options.D: Rapid decline over weeks to months

pretest.rationale: The hallmark course of vascular dementia is STEPWISE DECLINE: periods of relatively stable cognitive function interrupted by discrete steps down in function corresponding to new vascular events (strokes or TIAs). Between vascular events, the patient may be stable or even show minor improvement (cerebral plasticity and rehabilitation). This is in direct contrast to AD (gradual continuous decline) and LBD (fluctuating within days), and is not a rapid decline as in CJD.

distractorRationales.A: Gradual, insidious progression describes Alzheimer's disease.
distractorRationales.B: Dramatic hour-to-hour fluctuation is the hallmark of Lewy body dementia (DLB).
distractorRationales.D: Rapid decline over weeks to months suggests CJD (Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease) or other rapidly progressive encephalopathies.

pretest.hint: VaD = stairs down (discrete steps). AD = slope down (gradual). LBD = elevator (up and down).
pretest.clinicalPearl: The stepwise course of VaD means that aggressive risk factor management between events literally can flatten the staircase — fewer strokes/TIAs = fewer steps down.
pretest.examStrategy: Course of dementia questions: VaD = stepwise. AD = gradual. LBD = fluctuating. Know these three patterns — they appear on virtually every dementia exam.
pretest.keyTakeaway: VaD course: stepwise deterioration with periods of stability — each step corresponds to a vascular event.

pretest.stem: A nurse is teaching a group of older adults about stroke prevention. Which risk factor should be identified as the SINGLE STRONGEST modifiable risk factor for vascular dementia and stroke?

options.A: High body mass index
options.B: Sedentary lifestyle
options.C: Hypertension
options.D: Hyperlipidemia

pretest.rationale: Hypertension is the single strongest modifiable risk factor for both stroke and vascular dementia. Chronic hypertension causes lipohyalinosis (pathological thickening and damage) of small cerebral arteries, leading to white matter disease, lacunar infarcts, and progressive cognitive decline. Control of BP to < 130/80 mmHg reduces stroke risk by approximately 40% and is the most impactful preventive intervention for vascular dementia. Obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and hyperlipidemia are all significant modifiable risk factors, but hypertension is ranked highest in epidemiological studies.

distractorRationales.A: Obesity is a modifiable risk factor but does not carry the magnitude of cerebrovascular risk as hypertension.
distractorRationales.B: Sedentary lifestyle is modifiable and contributes significantly to vascular risk, but is not the single strongest factor.
distractorRationales.D: Hyperlipidemia is an important modifiable risk factor, but its effect size is smaller than hypertension for cerebrovascular disease specifically.

pretest.hint: Which one risk factor affects every blood vessel in the body every second of every day?
pretest.clinicalPearl: Hypertension doubles the risk of dementia. For every 10 mmHg sustained increase in systolic BP, dementia risk increases by approximately 20%.
pretest.examStrategy: Risk factor ranking questions: hypertension = #1 for vascular dementia. AF = #1 cardioembolic source. Hypertension is the most testable risk factor association across all gerontological and cardiovascular exams.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Hypertension is the single strongest modifiable risk factor for vascular dementia — target BP < 130/80.

pretest.stem: A 72-year-old woman with vascular dementia is admitted to a rehabilitation unit following a right MCA ischemic stroke 1 week ago. The rehabilitation nurse notes she is not participating in therapy, has poor oral intake, and when directly asked says 'Everything feels hopeless — why am I even here?' She cries several times daily. Her PHQ-9 score is 17/27. Which nursing action is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Reassure her that feelings of hopelessness are expected after stroke and will resolve on their own
options.B: Recognize this as post-stroke depression requiring active treatment; notify the physician for antidepressant consideration and initiate psychosocial nursing interventions
options.C: Attribute the crying to pseudobulbar affect and provide patient education about the neurological basis
options.D: Limit stimulation and activity to reduce emotional distress; discontinue rehabilitation until mood improves

pretest.rationale: Post-stroke depression (PSD) affects 30–50% of stroke patients. PHQ-9 of 17/27 = moderately severe depression (PHQ-9 15–19 = moderately severe). PSD is caused by BOTH psychological response (loss of function, role changes) AND structural brain injury (ischemic damage to frontal-limbic circuits). It does NOT reliably resolve without treatment. Active management includes: (1) SSRI initiation (sertraline or escitalopram) — proven effective in PSD; (2) psychotherapy (CBT or IPT when cognitively feasible); (3) rehabilitation participation (has antidepressant effect); (4) social support. Withholding rehabilitation worsens outcomes — exercise and purposeful activity are therapeutic. Simply waiting for spontaneous resolution is inappropriate with a PHQ-9 of 17.

distractorRationales.A: PSD does not reliably resolve spontaneously; waiting is inappropriate when PHQ-9 indicates moderately severe depression.
distractorRationales.C: While PBA may coexist, a PHQ-9 of 17 with consistent hopelessness and poor function is clinical depression requiring treatment — it cannot be explained as PBA alone.
distractorRationales.D: Limiting rehabilitation is contraindicated — exercise and purposeful activity are THERAPEUTIC for depression and improve recovery outcomes.

pretest.hint: PHQ-9 ≥ 15 = moderately severe depression. Post-stroke depression requires active treatment — not waiting.
pretest.clinicalPearl: Untreated post-stroke depression is associated with worse functional recovery, higher mortality, reduced rehabilitation participation, and poorer quality of life. SSRI treatment improves rehabilitation outcomes even beyond mood effects.
pretest.examStrategy: Post-stroke depression questions: PHQ-9 score + hopelessness + poor rehabilitation participation = active treatment required. Never 'wait and see' with PHQ-9 ≥ 15.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Post-stroke depression affects 30–50% of stroke patients; PHQ-9 ≥ 15 = moderately severe; requires SSRI + psychosocial intervention + continued rehabilitation.

pretest.stem: A nurse is creating a teaching plan for a 77-year-old man with moderate vascular dementia, his wife, and their adult daughter. Which content is MOST important to include? Select all that apply.

options.A: FAST stroke recognition (Face, Arm, Speech, Time to call 911) and TIA symptom recognition
options.B: Importance of daily blood pressure monitoring and antihypertensive medication adherence
options.C: Explanation that vascular dementia always progresses at the same rate as Alzheimer's disease
options.D: Executive dysfunction: the patient may struggle with planning and organization despite appearing alert
options.E: Fall prevention strategies addressing gait disturbance and orthostatic hypotension
options.F: Aggressive risk factor management can slow or prevent further cognitive decline in VaD

pretest.rationale: (A) FAST is essential — every new TIA or stroke = a potential step-down. Family must recognize and respond urgently. (B) BP adherence is the highest priority secondary prevention intervention. (C) is incorrect — VaD has a stepwise course that is potentially modifiable, unlike AD's gradual progression. (D) Executive dysfunction education prevents misinterpretation as stubbornness or non-compliance. (E) Fall prevention is paramount given early gait disturbance. (F) This crucial message distinguishes VaD from AD and motivates family engagement in prevention.

distractorRationales.C: VaD does NOT progress at the same rate as AD. It has a different pattern (stepwise vs. gradual) and can be modified by risk factor management — this is a key and hopeful distinction for families.

pretest.hint: VaD family teaching priorities: FAST, BP, executive function explanation, falls, and the modifiable nature of VaD progression.
pretest.clinicalPearl: Telling families that VaD progression can be influenced by their actions (BP control, medication adherence, lifestyle) is both accurate and motivating — it contrasts powerfully with the helplessness families feel with AD.
pretest.examStrategy: VaD family teaching SATA: FAST + BP adherence + executive dysfunction education + fall prevention + risk factor modifiability = all correct. Same rate as AD = incorrect.
pretest.keyTakeaway: VaD family teaching: FAST recognition, BP adherence, executive dysfunction explanation, fall prevention, and aggressive risk factor management can slow progression.

pretest.stem: A nurse is reviewing brain MRI reports for older patients with cognitive impairment. Which MRI finding is MOST consistent with subcortical vascular dementia?

options.A: Hippocampal and entorhinal cortex atrophy bilaterally
options.B: Diffuse cortical atrophy affecting all lobes with ventricular enlargement
options.C: Multiple small lacunar infarcts and extensive white matter hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR sequences
options.D: Large focal atrophy of the frontal and temporal lobes bilaterally

pretest.rationale: Subcortical vascular dementia (small vessel disease) produces characteristic MRI findings: (1) lacunar infarcts — small (5–20 mm) T1 hypointense / T2 hyperintense lesions in basal ganglia, thalamus, internal capsule, and periventricular white matter; (2) white matter hyperintensities (leukoaraiosis) — T2/FLAIR hyperintense periventricular and deep white matter changes reflecting chronic small vessel ischemia. These findings directly correspond to the executive dysfunction and gait disturbance of subcortical VaD. Hippocampal atrophy is AD's signature MRI finding. Diffuse cortical atrophy is seen in severe AD. Frontotemporal atrophy characterizes FTD.

distractorRationales.A: Hippocampal and entorhinal cortex atrophy = Alzheimer's disease signature on MRI.
distractorRationales.B: Diffuse cortical atrophy = severe Alzheimer's disease MRI pattern.
distractorRationales.D: Frontotemporal lobar atrophy = frontotemporal dementia (FTD) MRI pattern.

pretest.hint: VaD small vessel disease = lacunar infarcts + white matter changes on T2/FLAIR.
pretest.clinicalPearl: White matter hyperintensities on MRI can be present in normal aging (mild periventricular changes are common after age 65), but EXTENSIVE white matter disease correlating with cognitive and gait symptoms is pathological VaD.
pretest.examStrategy: MRI pattern recognition: VaD = lacunar infarcts + white matter hyperintensities. AD = hippocampal atrophy. FTD = frontotemporal atrophy. Know these associations.
pretest.keyTakeaway: Subcortical VaD MRI: lacunar infarcts + T2/FLAIR white matter hyperintensities in deep white matter, basal ganglia, thalamus.

posttest.stem: Which pathological mechanism MOST directly causes the white matter changes seen in subcortical vascular dementia?

options.A: Accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques in white matter axons
options.B: Lipohyalinosis of small deep perforating arteries from chronic hypertension causing ischemic white matter injury
options.C: Loss of cholinergic neurons in the nucleus basalis of Meynert
options.D: Accumulation of alpha-synuclein in white matter oligodendrocytes

posttest.rationale: Subcortical VaD white matter disease is caused by chronic hypertension (and diabetes) causing lipohyalinosis — pathological thickening and hyaline deposition in the walls of small deep perforating arteries. This narrowing reduces blood flow to the periventricular and deep white matter, causing ischemic demyelination and axonal injury seen as T2/FLAIR hyperintensities. Beta-amyloid is AD pathology; cholinergic neuron loss is the AChEI target in AD; alpha-synuclein is LBD pathology.

distractorRationales.A: Beta-amyloid affects synaptic function and forms plaques — not the primary cause of white matter ischemia.
distractorRationales.C: Cholinergic neuron loss causes the memory and attention deficits of AD — not white matter disease.
distractorRationales.D: Alpha-synuclein is the LBD/PD neuropathological protein — not involved in VaD white matter injury.

posttest.hint: Chronic hypertension damages small vessel walls → small vessel disease → white matter ischemia.
posttest.clinicalPearl: Lipohyalinosis is the structural link between hypertension and white matter disease — every year of uncontrolled hypertension silently damages cerebrovascular integrity.
posttest.examStrategy: Pathophysiology question: hypertension → lipohyalinosis → small vessel disease → white matter ischemia → subcortical VaD. Know this chain.
posttest.keyTakeaway: Chronic hypertension causes lipohyalinosis of small cerebral arteries → ischemic white matter damage → subcortical VaD.

posttest.stem: A 76-year-old woman with known vascular dementia is being discharged from hospital after a TIA. Her anticoagulation medication is apixaban 5 mg PO twice daily for atrial fibrillation. Which instruction is MOST important to include in her discharge teaching?

options.A: Take apixaban once daily in the morning with breakfast
options.B: Apixaban may be stopped if she has a fall without injury
options.C: Take apixaban 5 mg twice daily at the same times each day; never stop without physician guidance; report unusual bleeding or any fall with head injury immediately
options.D: Aspirin can be substituted for apixaban if she runs out of medication

posttest.rationale: Correct apixaban discharge teaching: (1) Twice daily dosing at consistent times — adherence is critical for continuous stroke protection; (2) Never stop without physician guidance — abrupt discontinuation dramatically increases short-term stroke risk; (3) Report bleeding signs (black/tarry stools, blood in urine, unusual bruising, prolonged bleeding); (4) Fall with head injury — report IMMEDIATELY even if feeling fine (ICH can develop slowly). Apixaban is taken TWICE daily (not once). It should NOT be stopped without medical guidance after falls without injury. Aspirin is NOT equivalent to apixaban for cardioembolic stroke prevention.

distractorRationales.A: Apixaban for AF is dosed twice daily — once daily is incorrect and would result in sub-therapeutic anticoagulation.
distractorRationales.B: Stopping anticoagulation after a fall without injury is inappropriate — the stroke risk immediately rebounds. Any change requires physician assessment.
distractorRationales.D: Aspirin is not an adequate substitute for anticoagulation in AF — it does not prevent cardioembolic strokes effectively.

posttest.hint: Apixaban teaching: twice daily, consistent times, never stop without MD guidance, report bleeding and head injury.
posttest.clinicalPearl: DOAC adherence education is one of the highest-impact nursing discharge teaching interventions in VaD/AF — missing doses creates daily windows of stroke vulnerability.
posttest.examStrategy: DOAC discharge teaching: twice daily dosing, never self-discontinue, bleeding signs, head injury reporting. These four elements appear repeatedly on NCLEX medication teaching questions.
posttest.keyTakeaway: Apixaban teaching: twice daily at consistent times, never stop without MD guidance, report bleeding and head injury.

posttest.stem: A 73-year-old man with moderate vascular dementia has been referred to a physiotherapist because of 'worsening walking.' The physiotherapist notes he walks with very small shuffling steps, barely clears the ground, and takes small rapid steps trying to stop (festination). What does this gait pattern MOST likely indicate?

options.A: Age-related deconditioning requiring strengthening exercises only
options.B: Frontal gait disorder (marche à petit pas) characteristic of subcortical vascular dementia
options.C: Parkinsonism consistent with concurrent Lewy body dementia
options.D: Peripheral neuropathy causing sensory ataxia

posttest.rationale: The described gait — small shuffling steps, minimal floor clearance, festination — is characteristic of frontal gait disorder (marche à petit pas, French for 'march of small steps'). This is a classic feature of subcortical vascular dementia caused by disruption of frontal-subcortical white matter circuits that control motor programming and gait initiation. Key differentiating features from Parkinsonian gait: (1) in VaD frontal gait, arm swing may be relatively preserved (unlike PD where arm swing is reduced); (2) VaD gait does not significantly improve with levodopa; (3) VaD gait often coexists with urinary urgency (frontal lobe release sign).

distractorRationales.A: This specific gait pattern is not age-related deconditioning — it has a specific neurological basis in subcortical pathology.
distractorRationales.C: While PD/LBD can produce similar-appearing gait, this patient has a known VaD diagnosis and the gait pattern is characteristic of VaD frontal gait disorder.
distractorRationales.D: Sensory ataxia produces a wide-based, stamping gait due to loss of proprioception — different from the narrow-based, shuffling frontal gait.

posttest.hint: Shuffling small steps + minimal clearance + festination in VaD = frontal gait disorder.
posttest.clinicalPearl: The frontal gait disorder of VaD responds to physiotherapy gait training and appropriate assistive devices (rollator walker) — not to levodopa as in PD.
posttest.examStrategy: Gait pattern recognition: VaD = frontal gait (shuffling, small steps); PD/LBD = Parkinsonian gait (shuffling, reduced arm swing, responds to levodopa); sensory ataxia = wide-based, stamping, worsens with eyes closed.
posttest.keyTakeaway: Frontal gait disorder (marche à petit pas) in VaD: shuffling, small steps, festination — physiotherapy + assistive device, not levodopa.

posttest.stem: A nurse is reviewing the medical history of a 71-year-old woman with newly diagnosed vascular cognitive impairment (mild VCI) with no current dementia. Which of the following findings represent MODIFIABLE risk factors that should be actively addressed to prevent progression to vascular dementia? Select all that apply.

options.A: Atrial fibrillation — currently not anticoagulated
options.B: Female sex
options.C: Hypertension (BP 162/94 on two readings)
options.D: HbA1c 9.2% (poorly controlled type 2 diabetes)

posttest.rationale: (A) AF is a major cardioembolic stroke risk that is modified by anticoagulation. Not being anticoagulated despite AF is an actionable risk. (C) Uncontrolled hypertension (BP 162/94) is the highest-priority modifiable risk — aggressive treatment required. (D) HbA1c 9.2% = poorly controlled diabetes — a major driver of microvascular brain injury; optimization reduces progression risk. (F) Active smoking accelerates atherosclerosis and is modifiable with cessation support. Female sex (B) and age 71 (E) are NON-MODIFIABLE risk factors.

distractorRationales.B: Female sex is a non-modifiable risk factor — cannot be changed.
distractorRationales.E: Age is the strongest non-modifiable risk factor for dementia — cannot be changed.

posttest.hint: Modifiable = something you can actively treat or change. Sex and age are fixed.
posttest.clinicalPearl: Mild VCI is the highest-yield window for secondary prevention — this is the stage where aggressive intervention can prevent progression to dementia. The modifiable risk factors must ALL be addressed simultaneously.
posttest.examStrategy: Modifiable vs non-modifiable VaD risk factors: AF, HTN, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, obesity = modifiable. Age, sex, genetics = non-modifiable.
posttest.keyTakeaway: Modifiable VaD risk factors: HTN, AF (anticoagulate), diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, obesity. Age and sex are non-modifiable.

posttest.stem: A 69-year-old man presents to a primary care clinic with his wife, who reports: 'He's been having more trouble managing our finances over the past year. He can't balance the checkbook anymore and gets confused with the bills. But his memory for events seems okay — he can tell you what we did last weekend.' Neuropsychological testing shows impaired executive function, processing speed, and working memory with relatively preserved episodic memory. MoCA is 22/30 (losing points on Trails B, clock drawing, digit span). He has poorly controlled hypertension and had a TIA 18 months ago. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.A: Early Alzheimer's disease with predominant memory impairment
options.B: Frontotemporal dementia with behavioral variant
options.C: Mild VCI (vascular cognitive impairment) due to subcortical small vessel disease
options.D: Normal aging with anxiety about cognitive function

posttest.rationale: Classic mild VCI profile: (1) executive function disproportionately impaired (finances, bill management) while episodic memory is relatively preserved (can recall last weekend); (2) neuropsychological pattern: executive, processing speed, working memory impaired > episodic recall; (3) MoCA 22 (below 26) with specific executive domain failures (Trails B, clock drawing, digit span); (4) significant vascular risk factor burden (poorly controlled HTN) + prior TIA; (5) functional impact limited to complex IADLs (finances) — IADLs requiring executive function affected before memory-dependent IADLs. Does NOT meet criteria for major VCI (dementia) as overall functional independence is preserved.

distractorRationales.A: AD presents with episodic memory impairment first — this patient's memory is relatively preserved, which argues against AD as the primary diagnosis.
distractorRationales.B: FTD behavioral variant presents with personality/behavioral changes before executive dysfunction — no behavioral symptoms are described here.
distractorRationales.D: MoCA of 22 with specific domain failures and vascular risk factors represents genuine cognitive impairment, not normal aging or anxiety.

posttest.hint: Executive function first, memory spared + vascular risk factors + TIA history = VCI pattern.
posttest.clinicalPearl: Mild VCI is the pre-dementia stage with the greatest opportunity for secondary prevention. Every patient should receive aggressive risk factor management at this stage.
posttest.examStrategy: Mild VCI vs early AD: executive function first (VCI) vs memory first (AD). Vascular risk factors + TIA = VCI. Insidious memory complaints = AD.
posttest.keyTakeaway: Mild VCI: executive dysfunction first, memory preserved + vascular risk factors = VCI pattern requiring aggressive secondary prevention.

posttest.stem: A nurse observes that an 81-year-old woman with vascular dementia suddenly begins crying uncontrollably while watching a mildly sad movie clip. The crying lasts several minutes, out of proportion to the stimulus, then stops abruptly. When asked how she is feeling she says 'I'm fine, I don't even know why I was crying.' Which term BEST describes this phenomenon?

options.A: Major depressive episode
options.B: Grief reaction
options.C: Pseudobulbar affect (pathological laughing and crying)
options.D: Conversion disorder

posttest.rationale: Pseudobulbar affect (PBA), also called pathological laughing and crying or emotionalism, is characterized by: (1) sudden, uncontrollable episodes of crying or laughing; (2) disproportionate to the emotional stimulus; (3) the patient often has little control and may not feel the underlying emotion; (4) abrupt onset and offset; (5) caused by structural disruption of corticobulbar pathways that modulate emotional expression. This is a neurological symptom of VaD (and ALS, MS, TBI, etc.) — NOT a psychiatric diagnosis or voluntary behavior. Treatment: dextromethorphan/quinidine (Nuedexta, FDA-approved); SSRIs at lower doses also have evidence.

distractorRationales.A: Major depression is characterized by persistent pervasive mood symptoms over weeks — not discrete uncontrollable crying episodes that resolve abruptly and are out of proportion.
distractorRationales.B: Grief reactions are purposeful emotional responses to recognized loss — this episode was disproportionate to a mild movie stimulus.
distractorRationales.D: Conversion disorder involves neurological symptoms (paralysis, blindness) from psychological stress — not the neurological pattern of PBA.

posttest.hint: Sudden, uncontrollable, disproportionate crying that resolves abruptly + neurological disease = PBA.
posttest.clinicalPearl: PBA is frequently misdiagnosed as depression — leading to inappropriate antidepressant prescribing at higher doses than needed. The key clinical feature is the episodic, disproportionate, and ego-dystonic (patient doesn't feel the emotion) nature of PBA.
posttest.examStrategy: PBA vs depression: PBA = sudden, brief, disproportionate, patient may not feel the emotion; depression = persistent, proportionate to circumstances, consistent low mood. Know this distinction.
posttest.keyTakeaway: PBA: sudden uncontrollable disproportionate crying/laughing, brief episodes, neurological origin — treat with Nuedexta or SSRIs; not major depression.

posttest.stem: Which cognitive domain is MOST severely and EARLIEST affected in vascular dementia?

options.A: Episodic memory (recall of recent events)
options.B: Executive function (planning, organizing, problem-solving)
options.C: Language (naming, word finding)
options.D: Visuospatial function (copying figures, spatial navigation)

posttest.rationale: Executive function is the cognitive domain most prominently and earliest affected in subcortical vascular dementia. This reflects the specific anatomical damage in VaD — subcortical white matter disease disrupts frontal-subcortical circuits that subserve executive processes (planning, sequencing, task-switching, problem-solving, working memory). Patients may appear relatively alert and able to hold a conversation, but cannot plan a meal, manage finances, or follow multi-step instructions. Episodic memory is affected but relatively less severely than in AD. Language and visuospatial function are less prominent early features of subcortical VaD.

distractorRationales.A: Episodic memory is the domain FIRST and MOST severely affected in Alzheimer's disease — not VaD.
distractorRationales.C: Language is prominently affected in aphasia syndromes from left hemisphere strokes and in frontotemporal dementia — but less characteristic of subcortical VaD.
distractorRationales.D: Visuospatial function is a prominent early feature of posterior cortical atrophy (a variant of AD) — not subcortical VaD.

posttest.hint: VaD = subcortical damage = frontal circuits = executive function first.
posttest.clinicalPearl: The executive function deficit in VaD creates a clinical presentation that can be mistaken for non-compliance or stubbornness — the patient may understand what needs to be done but cannot organize the steps to do it.
posttest.examStrategy: Domain-first questions: VaD = executive function first. AD = episodic memory first. FTD = behavior/personality first. Know these three.
posttest.keyTakeaway: VaD: executive function is the earliest and most prominently affected cognitive domain.

posttest.stem: A nurse is educating the family of an 80-year-old man with moderate vascular dementia about the differences between VaD and Alzheimer's disease. Which statements about vascular dementia are CORRECT? Select all that apply.

options.A: Vascular dementia tends to decline in a stepwise pattern following vascular events
options.B: Vascular dementia and Alzheimer's disease have identical presentations and cannot be distinguished clinically
options.C: Aggressive management of blood pressure, blood sugar, and atrial fibrillation can slow the progression of vascular dementia
options.D: Gait disturbance and urinary urgency are common early features of vascular dementia
options.E: Vascular dementia always responds well to acetylcholinesterase inhibitors
options.F: Post-stroke or post-TIA depression affects 30–50% of patients with vascular dementia

posttest.rationale: (A) Stepwise decline is the defining course feature of VaD. (C) Risk factor management is the most important intervention in VaD — it is modifiable, unlike AD. (D) Early gait disturbance and urinary urgency are characteristic subcortical VaD features — not shared with early AD. (F) Post-stroke depression is prevalent (30–50%) and requires active treatment. VaD and AD can often be distinguished clinically using onset, course, cognitive profile, and neurological examination (B is incorrect). AChEIs are NOT indicated and do not reliably benefit pure VaD (E is incorrect).

distractorRationales.B: VaD and AD have distinct clinical profiles — onset (stepwise vs. gradual), cognitive domain (executive vs. memory), neurological examination (focal signs in VaD vs. normal in early AD), and neuroimaging.
distractorRationales.E: AChEIs have inconsistent and limited evidence in pure VaD — they are NOT standard treatment for VaD and are not reliably effective.

posttest.hint: VaD characteristics: stepwise, modifiable, early gait/bladder symptoms, high depression prevalence. Not: identical to AD, and not reliably responsive to AChEIs.
posttest.clinicalPearl: The modifiability of VaD progression is the most hopeful and empowering message for families — their active participation in risk factor management truly makes a difference.
posttest.examStrategy: VaD facts SATA: stepwise + modifiable + early gait/bladder + depression = correct. Same as AD = incorrect. AChEI benefit = incorrect for pure VaD.
posttest.keyTakeaway: VaD: stepwise course, modifiable by risk factor management, early gait/bladder features, high depression prevalence — AChEIs not standard treatment.

posttest.stem: A 77-year-old woman with moderate vascular dementia is being managed in a community setting. Her medications include apixaban 5 mg twice daily (for AF), amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. Her last visit BP was 118/70 after starting amlodipine last month. This morning she appears confused and her BP is 96/58 in the office with HR 92 bpm. She is pale. What is the nurse's PRIORITY action?

options.A: Administer a scheduled dose of amlodipine as ordered
options.B: Reassess cognitive function with MoCA to determine if VaD has progressed
options.C: Withhold amlodipine; assess for bleeding (she is on anticoagulation); obtain orthostatic vitals; notify the physician urgently with SBAR
options.D: Increase oral fluid intake and reassess in 30 minutes

posttest.rationale: This patient has hemodynamic instability (BP 96/58, HR 92) with confusion in a patient who is anticoagulated. The priorities are: (1) WITHHOLD amlodipine — giving an antihypertensive to a patient with BP of 96/58 would cause dangerous hypotension; (2) ASSESS FOR BLEEDING — the patient is on apixaban; confusion + hypotension + tachycardia = possible GI bleed, internal hemorrhage — assess for black/tarry stools, blood in urine, bruising, abdominal tenderness; (3) ORTHOSTATIC VITALS — determine if orthostatic hypotension is contributing; (4) NOTIFY PHYSICIAN URGENTLY — this is a potential medical emergency. Administering amlodipine could be lethal. Cognitive assessment is inappropriate in a hemodynamically unstable patient. Waiting 30 minutes without assessment is dangerous.

distractorRationales.A: Administering amlodipine when BP is 96/58 would cause life-threatening hypotension — this is a critical medication safety error.
distractorRationales.B: Cognitive assessment is not the priority when the patient is hemodynamically unstable; MoCA is not a tool for acute assessment.
distractorRationales.D: Waiting without assessment and notification is inappropriate when a patient on anticoagulation presents with hypotension, tachycardia, and confusion.

posttest.hint: BP 96/58 + HR 92 + anticoagulated = possible bleeding emergency. What must you do first?
posttest.clinicalPearl: Anticoagulation-related GI bleeding can present with confusion (hypoperfusion) before overt bleeding signs are apparent. Always consider occult bleeding in anticoagulated patients with unexplained hemodynamic changes.
posttest.examStrategy: Hemodynamic instability + anticoagulation = hold the BP med, assess for bleeding, notify physician immediately. This type of clinical prioritization question appears frequently.
posttest.keyTakeaway: BP 96/58 + anticoagulated = withhold antihypertensive, assess for bleeding, notify physician urgently — this is a potential hemorrhagic emergency.

posttest.stem: A nurse is explaining to a patient with mild VCI why controlling blood pressure is so important for brain health. Which explanation is MOST accurate and evidence-based?

options.A: 'High blood pressure has no direct effect on the brain — it only affects the heart'
options.B: 'Blood pressure control is only important if you've already had a stroke'
options.C: 'Chronic high blood pressure damages the small blood vessels in the brain, causing tiny strokes and white matter injury that contribute to memory and thinking problems. Treating your blood pressure protects these vessels and may slow or prevent further cognitive decline'
options.D: 'Blood pressure control is important for cosmetic reasons — it prevents flushing of the face'

posttest.rationale: This explanation is accurate, physiologically grounded, and motivating: chronic hypertension causes lipohyalinosis of small cerebral arteries → lacunar infarcts + white matter ischemia → cognitive decline. The SPRINT-MIND trial demonstrated that intensive BP control (SBP < 120) reduced the risk of mild cognitive impairment by 19% and combined dementia + MCI by 15% compared to standard control. BP management is both primary prevention (before first event) and secondary prevention (after TIA/stroke).

distractorRationales.A: Hypertension has direct and major effects on cerebral vasculature — it is the leading cause of vascular brain injury.
distractorRationales.B: BP control is protective even without prior stroke — primary prevention evidence is strong.
distractorRationales.D: This is a medically inaccurate and trivializing explanation.

posttest.hint: Explain the mechanism: hypertension → small vessel damage → brain injury → cognitive impairment.
posttest.clinicalPearl: The SPRINT-MIND trial: intensive BP control (SBP < 120) reduced mild cognitive impairment risk — the first trial to demonstrate that treating BP can prevent cognitive impairment.
posttest.examStrategy: Patient teaching effectiveness questions: the best explanation is physiologically accurate, connects the intervention to the outcome, and is motivating (empowers the patient). Abstract explanations without connection to outcomes are less effective.
posttest.keyTakeaway: BP control protects cerebral small vessels from lipohyalinosis, reducing lacunar infarcts and white matter injury — directly preventing VCI progression.

flashcards.front: Vascular Dementia vs Alzheimer's Disease — Key Clinical Differences
flashcards.back:
VaD:
• STEPWISE decline (each step = vascular event)
• EXECUTIVE FUNCTION first (planning, organizing)
• Focal neurological signs present
• Early gait disturbance ('magnetic gait')
• Early urge incontinence
• Depression very common (30–50%)
• Modifiable by risk factor management
• MRI: lacunar infarcts + white matter changes

AD:
• GRADUAL continuous decline
• EPISODIC MEMORY first (recent recall)
• Neurological exam normal in early disease
• Gait preserved until late stages
• Incontinence late (Stage 6D)
• Depression in ~20%
• No disease-modifying treatment (except new DMTs)
• MRI: hippocampal atrophy
flashcards.hint: Stairs down (VaD) vs slow slope down (AD).
flashcards.explanation: The stepwise vs. gradual distinction is the single most important clinical differentiator between VaD and AD and is tested on virtually every dementia exam.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: VaD can be prevented and progression slowed — unlike AD. This is the most empowering clinical distinction for patients and families.

flashcards.back:
F — FACE: sudden facial drooping, asymmetric smile
A — ARM: sudden arm weakness (one arm drifts down)
S — SPEECH: sudden slurred speech, wrong words, inability to speak
T — TIME: call 911 IMMEDIATELY. Time is brain.

Additional symptoms:
• Sudden severe headache ('worst of life')
• Sudden vision loss (one or both eyes)
• Sudden balance/coordination problems

tPA window: 4.5 hours from symptom onset
Endovascular thrombectomy: up to 24 hours in selected patients
flashcards.hint: FAST = the most important stroke prevention teaching for VaD families.
flashcards.explanation: For every minute a stroke is untreated, 1.9 million brain cells die. Immediate recognition and emergency response is the most time-critical intervention in stroke care.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: TIA symptoms (stroke symptoms that resolve) are a medical emergency with 10–15% stroke risk in 90 days — FAST education applies to TIA as well as stroke.

flashcards.front: Vascular Risk Factor Hierarchy for VaD Prevention
flashcards.back:
1. HYPERTENSION (STRONGEST): target BP < 130/80; aggressive control
2. ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: anticoagulate with DOAC (apixaban preferred)
3. DIABETES: optimize glycemic control; HbA1c individualized (7–8% in older adults)
4. SMOKING: cessation at any age reduces risk
5. HYPERLIPIDEMIA: statin therapy; target LDL < 70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L)
6. PHYSICAL INACTIVITY: 150 min/week aerobic exercise
7. OBESITY: weight management

Non-Modifiable: age, sex, genetics (CADASIL)
flashcards.hint: HANDS: Hypertension, AF, No smoking, Diabetes, Statin.
flashcards.explanation: All modifiable risk factors must be addressed simultaneously — vascular risk is multiplicative, not additive.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Reducing SBP by 10 mmHg reduces stroke risk by approximately 20%. Consistent adherence to antihypertensive therapy is one of the highest-impact nursing teaching interventions.

flashcards.front: Antiplatelet vs Anticoagulation in Stroke Prevention
flashcards.back:
ANTIPLATELET (aspirin 81 mg or clopidogrel 75 mg):
• For NON-CARDIOEMBOLIC ischemic stroke/TIA
• Small vessel (lacunar) stroke
• Atherosclerotic large vessel stroke
• Dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel): 21 days after high-risk TIA/minor stroke

ANTICOAGULATION (DOAC preferred: apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran):
• For CARDIOEMBOLIC stroke (ATRIAL FIBRILLATION)
• Mechanical heart valve, hypercoagulable state
• Reduces AF stroke risk 60–70%
• Warfarin INR 2.0–3.0 if DOAC contraindicated
flashcards.hint: AF = anticoagulate. Lacunar/atherosclerotic = antiplatelet. Never anticoagulate lacunar.
flashcards.explanation: Anticoagulation for lacunar or small vessel infarction has no benefit and increases hemorrhagic risk — antiplatelet is the correct choice.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Aspirin for AF is NOT adequate stroke prevention — anticoagulation is significantly superior and aspirin provides minimal protection against cardioembolic strokes.

flashcards.back:
Classic subcortical VaD triad (Binswanger's disease / white matter disease type):
1. EXECUTIVE DYSFUNCTION: planning, organizing, problem-solving, multitasking
2. GAIT DISTURBANCE: marche à petit pas (small shuffling steps, magnetic gait)
3. URGE URINARY INCONTINENCE: early, before significant memory loss

Plus: psychomotor slowing, depression, pseudobulbar affect
Imaging: white matter hyperintensities (T2/FLAIR), lacunar infarcts
Cause: chronic hypertension → lipohyalinosis → small vessel disease
flashcards.hint: Executive + Gait + Incontinence = subcortical VaD triad.
flashcards.explanation: This triad distinguishes subcortical VaD from AD early in the disease course. Urinary incontinence in early cognitive impairment should always prompt investigation for vascular pathology.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: When an older adult presents with gait disturbance AND urinary urgency AND executive problems — think VaD (or NPH if adding ventricular dilation on imaging).

flashcards.back:
Prevalence: 30–50% of stroke and VaD patients
Mechanism: structural injury to frontal-limbic circuits + psychological response to disability
NOT just a 'normal reaction' — requires treatment

Screening:
• PHQ-9 at 1 month, 3 months, 6 months post-stroke
• GDS-15 in older adults

Treatment:
• SSRIs: sertraline 25–50 mg (first-line), escitalopram
• Venlafaxine: also evidence-based for PSD
• Psychotherapy: CBT/IPT when cognitively feasible
• Exercise: proven antidepressant effect
• Social engagement
• AVOID: TCAs (seizure threshold, cardiac effects, anticholinergic)
flashcards.hint: Post-stroke depression: 30–50%, treat with SSRI, do not wait for spontaneous resolution.
flashcards.explanation: Untreated PSD worsens functional recovery, reduces rehabilitation participation, and increases mortality. Active treatment improves multiple outcomes beyond mood.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Starting an SSRI for post-stroke depression improves rehabilitation participation and functional recovery even beyond its mood effects — it has dual benefit in stroke patients.

flashcards.back:
Apixaban (Eliquis): 5 mg TWICE DAILY; can take with or without food
Rivaroxaban (Xarelto): 20 mg ONCE DAILY with EVENING MEAL (food increases absorption)
Dabigatran (Pradaxa): 150 mg TWICE DAILY; do not crush/chew capsule

All DOACs:
• Take at consistent times every day
• NEVER stop without physician guidance (immediate stroke risk rebound)
• Report: unusual bruising, black tarry stools, blood in urine, prolonged bleeding
• Fall with head injury: report IMMEDIATELY even if feeling fine
• Avoid NSAIDs (increased GI bleed risk)
• Antidote available: andexanet alfa (for Xa inhibitors), idarucizumab (for dabigatran)
flashcards.hint: All DOACs: never stop without MD; report bleeding; head injury = emergency.
flashcards.explanation: DOAC education is one of the highest-stakes nursing discharge teaching tasks in VaD/AF management — adherence and safety monitoring are critical.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Missing just one dose of a DOAC creates a window of stroke vulnerability. Adherence tools (pill organizers, phone alarms, blister packs) significantly reduce missed doses.

flashcards.front: Pseudobulbar Affect (PBA) — Clinical Features and Treatment
flashcards.back:
Definition: sudden, uncontrollable, disproportionate episodes of laughing or crying
Neurological cause: injury to corticobulbar pathways controlling emotional expression
Key features:
• Episodic, abrupt onset and offset
• Disproportionate to stimulus (crying for an hour at a mildly sad commercial)
• Patient often does NOT feel the underlying emotion
• Ego-dystonic: patient aware the response is excessive

Distinguish from depression: PBA = episodic, brief, disproportionate; Depression = persistent, proportionate, consistent

Treatment:
• Dextromethorphan/quinidine (Nuedexta): FDA-approved for PBA
• SSRIs at low doses: also have evidence
• Education: reassure family this is neurological, not psychiatric
flashcards.hint: PBA = sudden disproportionate emotional episodes; neurological, not psychiatric; treat with Nuedexta.
flashcards.explanation: PBA is frequently misidentified as depression, leading to suboptimal treatment. The disproportionality and episodic nature are the distinguishing clinical features.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Family members of VaD patients with PBA experience significant distress watching sudden crying episodes — education that this is a neurological symptom (not grief, not suffering) provides significant relief.

flashcards.front: VaD MRI Findings — What to Expect
flashcards.back:
Subcortical (Small Vessel) VaD:
• T2/FLAIR white matter hyperintensities (periventricular, deep white matter)
• Lacunar infarcts: small (5–20 mm), T1 dark, T2 bright, in basal ganglia/thalamus/internal capsule/white matter
• Enlarged perivascular spaces

Cortical (Large Vessel) VaD:
• Cortical/subcortical infarctions in specific vascular territories
• Atrophy of affected territory

Strategic Infarct VaD:
• Small lesion in strategic location (thalamus, hippocampus, angular gyrus)
• Disproportionately large cognitive effect from small infarct

Compare: AD MRI = hippocampal atrophy; LBD = may be normal early
flashcards.hint: VaD MRI = white matter changes + lacunar infarcts. AD MRI = hippocampal atrophy.
flashcards.explanation: Understanding MRI findings helps nurses correlate neuroimaging reports with clinical presentations and communicate meaningfully with families about the evidence for the diagnosis.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Not all white matter changes on MRI are pathological VaD — mild periventricular changes are common in adults over 65. Extensive white matter disease correlating clinically is the key.

flashcards.front: Vascular Dementia — Primary vs Secondary Prevention
flashcards.back:
PRIMARY PREVENTION (before first stroke/TIA):
• BP control < 130/80
• Treat AF (anticoagulate)
• Optimize diabetes (HbA1c)
• Statin therapy
• Smoking cessation
• Exercise 150 min/week
• Weight management

SECONDARY PREVENTION (after stroke/TIA/established VCI):
• All primary prevention measures (even more aggressive)
• Antiplatelet OR anticoagulation (based on mechanism)
• FAST education for patients and families
• Carotid endarterectomy if indicated
• Cardiac monitoring for occult AF
• BP monitoring: daily at home for high-risk patients
flashcards.hint: Prevention is the treatment for VaD — both before and after the first event.
flashcards.explanation: In VaD, the nurse's most powerful therapeutic interventions are education-based and risk factor management-focused — more so than pharmacological cognitive treatment.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Secondary prevention after TIA/minor stroke has the highest return on investment — the 90-day post-event period is the highest-risk window where intervention is most impactful.

flashcards.front: Executive Dysfunction in VaD — Clinical Implications for Nursing
flashcards.back:
Executive dysfunction = impaired planning, organizing, sequencing, problem-solving, decision-making, and task initiation

Clinical manifestations:
• Cannot manage medications (understands the need but cannot organize the process)
• Cannot manage finances (checkbook, bills, online banking)
• Cannot plan meals or cook sequential recipes
• Cannot initiate tasks without prompting
• Difficulty with novel situations; follows routine but cannot adapt

Nursing adaptations:
• Break tasks into single steps (not multi-step instructions)
• Provide structured routine (predictability compensates for planning deficits)
• External aids: medication organizer, checklist, smartphone reminders
• Offer structured choices (not open-ended)
• Financial safeguards: POA for property, automated payments
flashcards.hint: Executive dysfunction = can't organize/plan — simplify, structure, support.
flashcards.explanation: Family members often misinterpret executive dysfunction as stubbornness or laziness. Education that it reflects specific neural circuit damage changes the family's emotional response from frustration to compassion.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: A patient with executive dysfunction can often still KNOW what they need to do — they simply cannot initiate or organize the steps. Prompting and cueing are the nursing interventions, not instruction.

flashcards.back:
CADASIL = Cerebral Autosomal Dominant Arteriopathy with Subcortical Infarcts and Leukoencephalopathy
Gene: NOTCH3 (chromosome 19) — autosomal dominant; 50% inheritance risk per child

Clinical features:
• Onset: 30s–50s (early-onset stroke and dementia)
• Recurrent TIAs and ischemic strokes without typical vascular risk factors
• Progressive white matter disease on MRI (anterior temporal poles: pathognomonic)
• Migraine with aura (often first symptom)
• Vascular dementia developing over decades
• Psychiatric symptoms (depression, anxiety, apathy)

Diagnosis: genetic testing (NOTCH3 mutation); skin biopsy (electron microscopy)
No disease-specific treatment; vascular risk factor management
Genetic counseling: all first-degree relatives
flashcards.hint: CADASIL = young-onset vascular dementia with migraine aura; NOTCH3 mutation; autosomal dominant.
flashcards.explanation: CADASIL is the most common monogenic cause of ischemic stroke and vascular dementia. It should be suspected in patients with young-onset stroke without typical vascular risk factors and a family history.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Anterior temporal white matter changes on MRI are highly specific for CADASIL — this is the pathognomonic MRI finding that distinguishes it from hypertensive white matter disease.
